title: Optimising total RNA quality and quantity by phenol-chloroform extraction method from human visceral adipose tissue: A standardisation study
author: Roy, Tomo, et al.

steps.title: Pre-preparation
steps.instruction: •Autoclave all glassware and surgical instruments (scalpel, scissor, forceps) before use. Pre-identify and label the microcentrifuge tubes.•Immediately submerge the VAT samples, collected from freshly excised abdominal fat tissue, into the sterile container in 1% PBS and transfer to the laboratory where extraction will take place.•Set the centrifuge to 4 °C well in advance.•Clean the working bench properly with 70% ethyl alcohol or any other decontaminant.

steps.title: Protocol
steps.instruction: (Optimisations and relevant justifications are mentioned in the statements marked with an *)

steps.title: Sample preparation and homogenisation
steps.instruction: 1. Wash the fresh tissue samples with 1% PBS solution and weigh it with the analytical balance.

steps.title: Sample preparation and homogenisation
steps.instruction: * We suggest a maximum of 500 mg of pure VAT. However, excised adipose tissue sample mass varies widely depending on the surgery and availability of VAT in the patient. Hence, we suggest to take the maximum amount of tissue available (not more than 500 mg) and adjust the TRIzol volume accordingly.

steps.title: Sample preparation and homogenisation
steps.instruction: 2. Dissect the samples accurately to ensure pure adipose tissue and mince it into fine pieces to provide large enough surface area for digestion reagent. Carefully put the minced tissue in a 2 mL tube and homogenise with homogeniser.

steps.title: Sample preparation and homogenisation
steps.instruction: We used a teflon homogenizer (GenetixBiotech), with a speed of 3000 rpm in a discontinuous manner, while allowing 5–10 s per stroke. The tubes were kept on ice while the process was being carried out.

steps.title: Sample preparation and homogenisation
steps.instruction: * Use a 2 mL tube for 500 mg VAT as it will provide ample space required for the homogenisation of the high tissue volume. However, when working with a lesser mass of VAT, use a 1.5 mL tube, because it makes the pipetting during the fat layer separation much more accessible.

steps.title: Sample preparation and homogenisation
steps.instruction: 3. Centrifuge the homogenate at 12,000 x g for 10 min.

steps.title: Sample preparation and homogenisation
steps.instruction: A fat layer will appear on the upper surface of the aqueous phase.

steps.title: Sample preparation and homogenisation
steps.instruction: 4. Carefully remove the fat layer by pipetting.

steps.title: Sample preparation and homogenisation
steps.instruction: * The manufacturer's protocol suggests to transfer the aqueous supernatant to a separate tube and proceed with the subsequent steps. However, they have used 1 mL TRIzol in their protocol. With the lesser volume of TRIzol reagent we are using (less than or equal to 250 μL), separating the supernatant will decrease the yield further and not always practically possible.

steps.title: Fat layer removal step and additional ethanol wash for improving RNA purity
steps.instruction: We evaluated whether the residual fat after the first centrifugation step interferes with pure RNA extraction by comparing A260/280 and A260/230 in samples processed with or without the fat layer removal step. While A260/280 did not show any significant changes, A260/230 ratio showed a significant increase towards the desired value compared to those without this step (Median [Q1-Q3] 1.78 [1.66–1.92] compared to 1.03 [0.34–1.22], p<0.001, Fig. 2).Fig. 2Effect of fat layer removal step. After the first centrifugation step, the fat layer was removed. Code 0=No additional step, 1=Additional step of fat removal. Boxplots show that a) 260/280 ratio undergoes no significant improvement (p = 0.14); b) 260/230 ratio has significant improvement with the additional step (p<0.001).* p<0.05 (Two sample Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction).Fig. 2

substeps.instruction: We evaluated whether the residual fat after the first centrifugation step interferes with pure RNA extraction by comparing A260/280 and A260/230 in samples processed with or without the fat layer removal step. While A260/280 did not show any significant changes, A260/230 ratio showed a significant increase towards the desired value compared to those without this step (Median [Q1-Q3] 1.78 [1.66–1.92] compared to 1.03 [0.34–1.22], p<0.001, Fig. 2).Fig. 2Effect of fat layer removal step. After the first centrifugation step, the fat layer was removed. Code 0=No additional step, 1=Additional step of fat removal. Boxplots show that
260/230 ratio has significant improvement with the additional step (p<0.001).* p<0.05 (Two sample Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction).Fig. 2

steps.title: Fat layer removal step and additional ethanol wash for improving RNA purity
steps.instruction: We tried two ethanol washing steps instead of a single washing to remove any remaining impurities in the form of salts. We observed that the A260/230 ratio showed significant improvement with two washing steps compared to one (Median [Q1-Q3] 1.76 [1.29–1.88] compared to 0.60 [0.24–1.09], p<0.001, Fig. 3).Fig. 3Effect of an additional ethanol wash after RNA precipitation. Code 1=single ethanol wash, 2=double ethanol wash. Boxplots show that a) 260/280 ratio undergoes no significant improvement (p = 0.76); b) 260/230 ratio has significant improvement with the additional step (p<0.001).* p<0.05 (Two sample Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction).Fig. 3

We observed that the A260/230 ratio showed significant improvement with two washing steps compared to one (Median [Q1-Q3] 1.76 [1.29–1.88] compared to 0.60 [0.24–1.09], p<0.001, Fig. 3).Fig. 3Effect of an additional ethanol wash after RNA precipitation. Code 1=single ethanol wash, 2=double ethanol wash. Boxplots show that
260/230 ratio has significant improvement with the additional step (p<0.001).* p<0.05 (Two sample Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction).Fig. 3

steps.title: Data analysis
steps.instruction: Data were analysed in R programming platform (version 3.5.3) using RStudio, and Orange toolkit [19], [20], [21]. We tested the variables for normality by graphical representation (histogram and Q-Q plot) and Shapiro-Wilk significance test. The 260/280 and 260/230 ratio were presented as median (quartiles Q1-Q3). We further tested the differences between groups. The comparisons for non-parametric variables were carried out using the Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test (for two groups) or Kruskal-Wallis test (more than two groups), and for parametric data, t-test was used. Post-hoc analysis for differences between groups was done using pairwise comparisons by Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Spearman's Rho test was used for the correlation between variables. For all analytical purposes, p-values less than 0.05 were considered to be statistically significant.
